title: Origami fortune teller
subtitle: Folding the four-pocket cootie catcher from a square sheet
excerpt: The fortune teller: also called a cootie catcher or chatterbox: is one of the most widely known paper folds. Starting from a square sheet, four corners are folded to the centre twice to create a structure with four pockets that open and close when the maker inserts a finger and thumb in each hand. The finished model can be decorated with words, numbers, or drawings.
sourceNotes: Traditional paper fold with origins in European paper folding practice, widely documented in children's craft and origami literature throughout the twentieth century. No single attributable source.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fold in which the paper is brought toward you, creating a concave crease: the paper forms a valley shape. In origami diagrams, shown as a dashed line with an arrow indicating the fold direction. The most common basic fold in origami.

content.text: The fortune teller is made up of

s only. All four construction folds take the corner of the square to a centre point, and the result is a square that is smaller and has four pockets pointing outward. Understanding the valley fold here provides a foundation for more complex origami, where the same fold is combined with mountain folds and reverse folds to create three-dimensional structures.

items.name: Square origami paper, 15 cm (slug: kami-15cm)
items.description: Any square paper works. Origami kami at 15 cm is a convenient size: large enough to handle easily, small enough that the finished model fits in the hand. Plain A4 paper trimmed to a square also works.

items.name: Mini bone folder (slug: origami-bone-folder-mini)
items.description: Optional. Useful for pressing the folds flat cleanly. For this model, finger pressure alone is sufficient.

content.text: Step 1: fold in half both ways

Place the square coloured side down. Fold the square in half from bottom to top: bring the bottom edge up to meet the top edge and press the crease flat. Unfold. Fold in half from left to right and press flat. Unfold. You now have two crease lines crossing at the centre of the square. These marks will guide the next step.
Step 2: fold all four corners to the centre

Fold one corner of the square precisely to the centre point where the two crease lines cross. Press the fold flat. Repeat for all four corners. The result is a smaller square, still coloured side down, with four triangular flaps meeting at the centre. This shape is called the blintz base in origami. Press all folds crisp before continuing.

Turn the blintz base over so the folded flaps face down. The surface now showing is plain paper. Fold all four corners of this smaller square to the centre point again. Press all folds flat. The model is now a still smaller square with four triangular flaps on the upward-facing side.

Fold the model in half: bring two opposite flaps toward each other to form a rectangle: and press flat. Unfold and fold in the other direction. You will notice that the model wants to open into a three-dimensional shape when this last fold is pressed. Insert a finger and thumb from each hand into the four pocket openings on the underside and push the model open. The four coloured triangles now face outward and the model can pinch open and closed in two directions.

Write numbers (1-8) on the eight inner triangular sections visible when the model is open. Write a word or short message on the underside of each inner flap. Colour the outer four triangles in four different colours or patterns. When used as a game, the player picks a colour, then a number, and the flap bearing that number is lifted to reveal the message beneath.
